title: The Software Landscape: Brokers, Charts, Data & Screeners
html:
Before you place a single trade you need to understand the stack of software that sits between you and the market. New traders often try to make one tool do everything, get frustrated, and blame themselves. The real problem is that the industry deliberately splits into four overlapping but distinct categories, and each is good at a different job. This lesson maps the whole landscape so the next seven lessons have a frame to hang on.

The four pillars

Almost every tool you will ever touch falls into one of these buckets, even when a single company sells more than one of them:

  • Broker platforms. The software that actually routes your orders to an exchange and holds your money. Examples: thinkorswim (Charles Schwab), Interactive Brokers (TWS), Webull, Tastytrade, Robinhood. This is the only category that can legally execute trades.
  • Charting / analysis platforms. Built for visualizing price and drawing on it. The dominant one is TradingView. They may connect to a broker for execution, but their core value is charts, indicators, alerts, and a scripting language.
  • Data providers. The wholesalers of the actual numbers — real-time quotes, historical bars, fundamentals, news. Examples: Polygon.io, IEX, Nasdaq, Refinitiv. You usually consume their data through a broker or charting tool, but if you build your own bots you will buy data directly.
  • Screeners / scanners. Tools that filter thousands of symbols down to a handful that match your rules (e.g. "gapped up 4%, above the 50-day average, volume > 1M"). Finviz, the TradingView Screener, and thinkorswim scans live here.
Tip.
The single biggest beginner mistake is paying for four subscriptions before knowing what each pillar does. Start with one free broker paper account and a free TradingView account. That covers all four pillars well enough to learn for months.

How they fit together

A realistic retail workflow chains the pillars in a pipeline. Data flows in, gets filtered, gets analyzed, then an order goes out:

workflow.txt
# The retail trading pipeline, left to right [ DATA PROVIDER ] --quotes/bars--> [ CHARTING + SCREENER ] Polygon TradingView | | | scan + analyze | v | [ ALERT fires ] | | v v [ BROKER PLATFORM ] <----place order---- [ YOU decide ] thinkorswim / IBKR | v [ EXCHANGE: NYSE / Nasdaq / CME ]

Notice that the chart and the broker are usually two different programs. Many pros analyze in TradingView and execute in thinkorswim or IBKR, because the charting tool is nicer but the broker's order routing and fills are what they trust with real money.

Free vs paid, and what actually matters

CapabilityFree tierWhen to pay
Delayed chartsYes, everywhereNever just for delayed data
Real-time quotesOften via your brokerIf your tool and broker differ
Multiple alerts1-2 alerts freeWhen you track many setups
Multi-chart layouts1 chart freeWhen you trade several markets
Scanning the whole marketLimited filtersWhen screening is your edge

Real-time data is the one place where money genuinely buys an edge for active traders, and even then your broker usually bundles it free for the products you can actually trade. Everything else — indicators, drawings, scripts — is essentially free.

Principle.
Pick your broker for execution quality, regulation, and the products you trade. Pick your charting tool for how it feels to look at all day. These are separate decisions and you should make them separately.

A note on data vendors disagreeing

Two charts of the same stock can show slightly different prices, volume, or even different daily highs. This is normal: each vendor aggregates from different exchanges and feeds, and crypto especially varies because every exchange is its own market. Always know which data source a chart is using before you act on a precise number, and always confirm the price in your broker before sending an order.

▸Try It

Goal: map your own current stack onto the four pillars.

  1. Write down every trading-related app or website you currently use.
  2. Label each one as broker, charting, data, or screener (some may wear two hats).
  3. Identify any pillar you have no tool for yet.
  4. Confirm which single tool can actually execute a trade — that is your broker, and only it touches real money.

title: A Full TradingView Tour: Charts, Timeframes & Drawing Tools
html:
TradingView is the most widely used charting platform in retail trading, and for good reason: it runs in any browser, the charts feel great, and almost everything you read about technical analysis assumes you are looking at one. This lesson is a guided tour of the chart surface itself — the canvas, the timeframe controls, the drawing tools — so that by the end you can navigate fluently without hunting through menus.

The chart anatomy

Open a chart and you are looking at five regions. Learn their names because every later lesson refers to them:

  • The price scale runs down the right edge. Click and drag it to compress or stretch the vertical axis. Double-click it to auto-fit.
  • The time scale runs along the bottom. Drag it left/right to zoom the horizontal axis; scroll to pan through history.
  • The top toolbar holds the symbol search, the timeframe selector, the chart type (candles, line, bars, Heikin Ashi), the indicators button, and alerts.
  • The left toolbar is all drawing tools — trend lines, horizontal levels, Fibonacci, text, shapes.
  • The right sidebar holds your watchlist, alerts panel, and the data window.
Tip.
Press / anywhere to jump to symbol search. Type a ticker like AAPL, or an exchange-qualified one like NASDAQ:AAPL or BINANCE:BTCUSDT, to avoid ambiguity between data sources.

Timeframes and how they nest

The timeframe (or interval) is how much time one candle represents. A 1D chart draws one candle per trading day; a 5 chart draws one per five minutes. The art of multi-timeframe analysis is reading several at once: the higher timeframe sets context and trend, the lower timeframe times your entry.

Interval codeEach candleTypical use
1, 5, 151 / 5 / 15 minDay trading, entries
60, 2401 hr / 4 hrSwing context
D1 dayThe default trend view
W, M1 week / 1 monthLong-term structure

Type a number then press Enter to jump to a minute interval, or type 1D, 1W directly. Star your most-used intervals so they appear as one-click buttons in the toolbar.

Drawing tools that actually matter

The left toolbar has dozens of tools, but four carry most of the load. Practice these until they are muscle memory:

  • Horizontal Line (hotkey: hold Alt and click, or pick from the toolbar) marks support, resistance, and round numbers. The most-used drawing in trading.
  • Trend Line connects swing highs or swing lows to visualize a slope. Hold Shift while drawing to snap to 45-degree angles.
  • Fib Retracement drops the classic 0.382 / 0.5 / 0.618 levels between a swing low and high to anticipate pullback zones.
  • Long/Short Position tool lets you visually set an entry, stop, and target and instantly see the risk/reward ratio — invaluable for planning a trade before you take it.
Hands-on.
Every drawing has a settings gear (double-click it) where you set color, line style, and — crucially — whether it extends right, stays on this timeframe only, or shows on all timeframes. A support level you want to respect on every chart should be set to show on all intervals.

Keyboard shortcuts that save hours

shortcuts.txt
# The shortcuts worth memorizing first / open symbol search Alt + W add current symbol to a watchlist Alt + A create an alert at the cursor price Alt + H draw a horizontal line Alt + T draw a trend line Alt + F draw a Fib retracement Ctrl + Z undo (yes, drawings undo too) , / . step backward / forward in bar-replay

That bar-replay mode (the rewind icon in the top toolbar) is a hidden gem: it hides future price and lets you step the chart forward bar by bar, so you can practice reading setups as they unfold without cheating by seeing the outcome.

Watch out.
A clean chart with a few meaningful lines beats a chart smothered in twenty trend lines and five indicators. Clutter creates false confidence. If you cannot explain why a drawing is on the chart, delete it.
▸Try It

Goal: navigate a chart fluently using only the keyboard and a few drawings.

  1. Press / and load NASDAQ:AAPL on the daily interval.
  2. Switch to the 1-hour, then the weekly, using the timeframe box — notice how the trend story changes.
  3. Draw one horizontal line at an obvious support level and set it to show on all timeframes.
  4. Open bar-replay, rewind 30 bars, and step forward one bar at a time to watch your level get tested.

title: Indicators, Watchlists, Saved Layouts & Multi-Chart
html:
A bare candlestick chart is only half a workspace. The other half is the set of indicators you overlay, the watchlists that feed you symbols, and the saved layouts that let you reopen a fully configured workspace in one click. This lesson turns TradingView from a chart viewer into a personal cockpit you can return to every day in the same state.

Adding and configuring indicators

Click Indicators in the top toolbar (or press the indicators hotkey) to open a searchable library. There are two flavors:

  • Overlays draw on top of price in the main pane — moving averages, Bollinger Bands, VWAP, Ichimoku.
  • Oscillators draw in a separate pane below price because their scale is different — RSI, MACD, Stochastics, volume.

Search for Moving Average and add it. Then hover the indicator's name on the chart and click the gear to open its settings. Every indicator has an Inputs tab (the numbers, like length 50) and a Style tab (colors and visibility). Add a second moving average and set its length to 200 to get the classic 50/200 pairing.

Tip.
Built-in indicators are tagged with a flask icon; community scripts are under the Community tab. Community scripts are unvetted code from strangers — useful for inspiration, but never trade a script whose logic you have not read. You will learn to write your own in the next lessons, which is the only way to fully trust one.

Watchlists: your symbol funnel

A watchlist is a saved list of tickers shown in the right sidebar. Click the symbol you want to chart and the main pane jumps to it. Build watchlists by theme, not by whim:

  • An index/context list: SPY, QQQ, the dollar index, the VIX, the 10-year yield. You glance at these first to read the day's weather.
  • A focus list: the 10-20 symbols you are actively stalking for a setup this week.
  • A holdings list: what you actually own, so risk is always one click away.

Add the current symbol with Alt + W. You can color-code rows (flag them) to group, say, longs in green and shorts in red. The watchlist also shows live percent-change columns so it doubles as a lightweight scanner.

Saved layouts and templates

Two different things get saved in TradingView and people confuse them constantly:

What it savesNameUse
Chart arrangement, symbols, drawings, indicatorsLayoutReopen a whole workspace
Just the set of indicators & their settingsIndicator TemplateApply your indicators to any new symbol

The flow is: build the perfect chart once, save the indicators as a template named something like swing-setup, then apply that template to any symbol in two clicks. Separately, save the whole multi-chart arrangement as a named layout so it survives a browser refresh and logging in from another machine.

Hands-on.
Cloud sync is the quiet superpower. Because layouts and templates live on TradingView's servers, your exact workspace follows you to any browser. Make this a habit: never leave a chart un-saved at the end of a session.

Multi-chart: seeing several markets at once

Paid tiers unlock multi-chart grids (2x2, 4x4, and more). The killer feature is chart linking: a small colored circle on each pane controls a sync group. Charts in the same group change symbol together, so a 2x2 grid can show one ticker on four timeframes simultaneously — change the symbol once and all four update.

multichart-setup.txt
# A practical 2x2 multi-timeframe layout, all linked +----------------------+----------------------+ | TOP-LEFT | TOP-RIGHT | | Weekly (W) | Daily (D) | | trend + 200 MA | trend + 50/200 MA | +----------------------+----------------------+ | BOTTOM-LEFT | BOTTOM-RIGHT | | 1-hour (60) | 5-min (5) | | structure | entry timing + VWAP | +----------------------+----------------------+ # Put all 4 panes in the same link group (same color circle). # Change symbol in ANY pane and all four jump together.

This single layout is most of what a discretionary swing trader needs: top-down context on the left, entry timing on the right, all driven from one symbol search. Save it as a layout and you reopen your entire analytical view instantly tomorrow.

Watch out.
Indicators are lagging summaries of price you can already see. Two or three you deeply understand will serve you better than ten you half-remember. If a new indicator does not change a decision, it is decoration.
▸Try It

Goal: build and save a reusable multi-timeframe cockpit.

  1. Add a 50 and 200 moving average plus the RSI to a daily chart; save them as an indicator template called core.
  2. Create a focus watchlist of five symbols you find interesting.
  3. Build a 2x2 layout with weekly, daily, 1-hour, and 5-minute panes, all in the same link group.
  4. Apply your core template and save the whole thing as a named layout, then refresh the browser to confirm it persists.

title: Screeners & Alerts: From Whole Market to One Ping
html:
You cannot watch five thousand symbols. The job of a screener is to filter the whole market down to the handful that match your rules, and the job of an alert is to tell you when one of those does something worth acting on — without you staring at a screen all day. Together they turn the market from an ocean into a short, actionable list.

The screener: filtering the market

TradingView's Screener (separate stock, crypto, and forex screeners) is a giant filterable table. You stack conditions and it returns every symbol that passes all of them right now. Filters fall into three families:

  • Descriptive: exchange, sector, market cap, average volume — narrow the universe to tradeable names.
  • Technical: price vs a moving average, RSI level, distance from a 52-week high, percent change today.
  • Fundamental: P/E, revenue growth, dividend yield — for longer-horizon ideas.

A practical momentum screen reads like a sentence: "US common stocks, average volume over one million, price above the 50-day average, up more than three percent today." That single screen can replace an hour of flipping charts. Save it, and re-run it every morning to get a fresh focus list you can drop straight into a watchlist.

Tip.
Build screens that are loose, not surgical. If a screen returns zero or two thousand results it is useless. Aim for 10-40 names so the list is short enough to eyeball but not so tight that you only catch setups that already played out.

Alerts: the four ingredients

Every alert is defined by four choices. Get these straight and you can build any alert in the platform:

IngredientQuestion it answersExample
ConditionWhat to watchPrice, an indicator value, a drawing
TriggerWhat event fires itCrossing, greater than, entering a channel
FrequencyHow often it may fireOnce, once per bar, once per bar close
NotificationHow you are toldApp push, email, popup, webhook

Price alerts vs indicator alerts

The simplest alert is a price level: press Alt + A at the cursor and you get a horizontal line that pings when price reaches it. But TradingView's real power is alerting on indicator conditions. In the alert dialog, the first dropdown can be set not just to the symbol's price but to any indicator on the chart — so you can fire when the RSI crosses above 70, when the 50 crosses above the 200 (a golden cross), or when MACD turns up.

Hands-on.
Crossing-type alerts almost always want frequency Once Per Bar Close, not Once Per Bar. Intrabar values flicker back and forth above and below a line and will spam you with false crosses; waiting for the bar to close confirms the signal and fires once.

Notification channels

An alert is worthless if you do not see it. TradingView can notify you through several channels at once:

  • App push to your phone — the default for most traders.
  • Email — a durable record you can search later.
  • On-screen popup + sound — for when you are at the desk.
  • Webhook — an HTTP POST to a URL you control, the bridge to automation (covered conceptually in the workflow lesson).

Alerting on a custom condition

The most flexible alerts come from your own Pine Script (next lessons). A script can expose an alertcondition that shows up in the alert dialog as a selectable trigger. Here is the smallest possible example so you can see how a script and an alert connect:

alert_demo.pine
//@version=5 indicator('RSI Alert Demo', overlay=false) len = input.int(14, 'RSI Length') r = ta.rsi(close, len) plot(r, 'RSI') hline(70) // expose two conditions to the alert dialog oversold = ta.crossunder(r, 30) overbought = ta.crossover(r, 70) alertcondition(overbought, 'RSI crossed above 70', '{{ticker}} RSI is overbought') alertcondition(oversold, 'RSI crossed below 30', '{{ticker}} RSI is oversold')

After adding this to a chart, opening the alert dialog and choosing the indicator reveals RSI crossed above 70 as a trigger. The {{ticker}} placeholder gets replaced with the real symbol in the message, so one alert template works across every chart.

Watch out.
Alerts are not orders. A fired alert means "look now," not "buy now." Treat every ping as a prompt to open the chart and apply your judgment. The day you start blindly trading alerts is the day a single bad alert design starts trading your account for you.
▸Try It

Goal: go from a market-wide screen to a single phone ping.

  1. Build a screen for liquid stocks up more than three percent today and above their 50-day average; save it.
  2. Pick one result and chart it.
  3. Set a price alert just above today's high with frequency Once Per Bar Close, delivered as an app push.
  4. Add the RSI Alert Demo script and create an overbought alert, then compare which fires first.

title: Pine Script Basics: Writing Your First Indicator
html:
Every indicator you have used was written in Pine Script, TradingView's own programming language. The reason to learn it is independence: instead of trusting a black-box community script, you write exactly the calculation you want, and you understand every line. This lesson writes a real, runnable indicator from an empty editor.

Opening the editor

At the bottom of any chart, click Pine Editor. It opens a code pane. Write or paste a script, then click Add to chart and it renders live on the bars above. Errors appear at the bottom with a line number. That tight loop — edit, add to chart, see result — is how you learn the language fastest.

The three required ingredients

Every Pine indicator has exactly three things: a version annotation, a declaration, and at least one output. The smallest valid indicator is three lines:

hello.pine
//@version=5 indicator('Hello Pine', overlay=true) plot(close)

Line by line: //@version=5 picks the language version (always put it first). indicator(...) declares this script as a study; overlay=true means draw on the price pane rather than a separate one. plot(close) draws the closing price as a line. That is a complete, working indicator.

Tip.
The difference between indicator() and strategy() is the whole next lesson. An indicator only draws and signals; a strategy can simulate buying and selling for backtests. Older code used the keyword study() — that is the v3/v4 name for indicator(); you will still see it in the wild.

The bar-by-bar execution model

The single most important concept: your script runs once per bar, left to right across history, then once on every new tick of the live bar. Variables like close, high, volume are series — on each bar they hold that bar's value. To look back, index with brackets: close[1] is the previous bar's close. This model is why Pine feels different from normal programming: you are describing what to compute on a single bar, and TradingView replays it across all of them.

Inputs: making it configurable

Hard-coded numbers are amateur hour. Wrap every tunable value in an input so users (including future you) can change it from the settings gear without touching code. Here is a real, useful indicator: a dual moving average with a shaded trend background.

dual_ma.pine
//@version=5 indicator('Dual MA Trend', overlay=true) // --- Inputs (appear in the settings gear) --- fastLen = input.int(20, 'Fast MA', minval=1) slowLen = input.int(50, 'Slow MA', minval=1) src = input.source(close, 'Source') // --- Calculations --- fast = ta.sma(src, fastLen) slow = ta.sma(src, slowLen) bullish = fast > slow // --- Outputs --- plot(fast, 'Fast', color=color.aqua, linewidth=2) plot(slow, 'Slow', color=color.orange, linewidth=2) // shade the background green when fast > slow, else red bgcolor(bullish ? color.new(color.green, 90) : color.new(color.red, 90)) // mark the exact crossover bars plotshape(ta.crossover(fast, slow), 'Cross Up', style=shape.triangleup, location=location.belowbar, color=color.green) plotshape(ta.crossunder(fast, slow), 'Cross Down', style=shape.triangledown, location=location.abovebar, color=color.red)

Add this to a chart and you get two moving averages, a faint colored background that flips with the trend, and triangles at the exact crossover bars. Every number is editable from the gear. This is already more useful and more trustworthy than most community scripts, because you wrote it.

Reading the namespaces

NamespaceHoldsExample
ta.Technical analysis mathta.sma, ta.rsi, ta.crossover
input.User-configurable inputsinput.int, input.source
color.Colors and transparencycolor.new, color.red
math.Plain math functionsmath.abs, math.max
Hands-on.
The ? in bullish ? a : b is the ternary operator: "if bullish then a else b." Pine has no multi-line if/else for assigning a plot color inline, so ternaries are everywhere. Read them as a compact if-statement.
Watch out.
A classic bug: referencing a series before it has enough history. On the first 50 bars, ta.sma(src, 50) returns na (not-a-number) because there are not yet 50 bars. Your script must tolerate na early values — never assume a value exists on bar one.
▸Try It

Goal: write and customize your own indicator from scratch.

  1. Paste dual_ma.pine into the Pine Editor and click Add to chart.
  2. Open the settings gear and change the fast MA to 10 and the slow to 30 without editing code.
  3. Add a third input that switches the moving average type between SMA and EMA (hint: input.string plus an if on ta.ema vs ta.sma).
  4. Confirm the crossover triangles still appear on the correct bars.

title: Pine Strategies & the Strategy Tester (and Overfitting)
html:
An indicator describes the market; a strategy trades it — in simulation. When you declare a script with strategy() instead of indicator(), TradingView unlocks the Strategy Tester: it replays your buy and sell rules across all historical bars and reports how a hypothetical account would have done. This is backtesting, and it is the most powerful — and most dangerous — feature in the platform.

From indicator to strategy

The only structural change is the declaration plus calls to strategy.entry and strategy.close (or strategy.exit). Here is a complete, runnable moving-average-crossover strategy:

ma_cross_strategy.pine
//@version=5 strategy('MA Cross Strategy', overlay=true, initial_capital=10000, default_qty_type=strategy.percent_of_equity, default_qty_value=100, commission_type=strategy.commission.percent, commission_value=0.05) // --- Inputs --- fastLen = input.int(20, 'Fast MA') slowLen = input.int(50, 'Slow MA') // --- Signals --- fast = ta.sma(close, fastLen) slow = ta.sma(close, slowLen) goLong = ta.crossover(fast, slow) goFlat = ta.crossunder(fast, slow) plot(fast, color=color.aqua) plot(slow, color=color.orange) // --- Orders --- if goLong strategy.entry('Long', strategy.long) if goFlat strategy.close('Long')

Add this to a chart and the Strategy Tester panel appears at the bottom with simulated trades plotted on the price. Crucially, notice the realism settings in the declaration: initial_capital, position sizing as a percent of equity, and a commission_value. A backtest with zero commission and unlimited size is a fantasy; always include costs.

Reading the Strategy Tester

The tester has tabs. The numbers that actually matter, and how to read them:

MetricWhat it tells youTrap
Net ProfitTotal simulated gainMeaningless without drawdown
Max DrawdownWorst peak-to-trough lossThis is the pain you must survive
Profit FactorGross profit / gross loss<1.3 is fragile
Total TradesSample size<30-50 trades proves nothing
Percent ProfitableWin rateHigh win rate can still lose money
Principle.
Judge a strategy by risk-adjusted results, not raw profit. A system that doubled the account but suffered a 60% drawdown is untradeable by a human — you would have quit at the bottom. Look at max drawdown and trade count before you look at net profit.

The overfitting trap

Here is the trap that ruins beginners: you tweak the fast and slow lengths, watch net profit climb, and feel like a genius. You have not found an edge — you have curve-fit the parameters to this exact slice of history. The strategy memorized the past instead of learning a rule. On new data it falls apart.

Watch out.
Three signatures of overfitting: (1) the optimal parameters are oddly specific (fast=23, slow=61); (2) results collapse if you nudge a parameter by one; (3) it works on one symbol but not its peers. A robust edge is a broad plateau of "good enough" settings, not a single fragile peak.

Defending against it

  • Out-of-sample testing. Optimize on the first 70% of history, then test once on the untouched last 30%. If it dies out of sample, it was overfit.
  • Demand a large sample. A few dozen trades can be luck. Hundreds of trades across different market regimes is evidence.
  • Test across symbols. A real edge usually shows up, weaker but present, on related instruments.
  • Prefer fewer parameters. Every input you add is another knob to accidentally overfit. Two parameters are safer than seven.
Hands-on.
Beware lookahead bias and repainting. If your strategy uses request.security on a higher timeframe or references future bars, the backtest can "see" data it would not have had live, inflating results. The Strategy Tester also fills orders on the next bar's open by default — realistic — but signals computed on the live, unclosed bar can flip, so confirm on bar close.
Tip.
A backtest is a hypothesis, not a guarantee. Past performance famously does not predict future results, and a clean equity curve in the tester is the start of validation, never the end. The honest next step is forward-testing in paper trading, which is exactly where the next lessons go.
▸Try It

Goal: run a backtest and deliberately catch yourself overfitting.

  1. Add ma_cross_strategy.pine to a liquid stock on the daily timeframe and read the net profit and max drawdown.
  2. Tune the fast/slow lengths to maximize net profit and note the "best" values.
  3. Now switch the chart to a different but similar stock without changing the parameters — watch the results likely fall apart.
  4. Write one sentence explaining why step 3 is evidence of overfitting.

title: Broker Platforms: thinkorswim, thinkScript & the Majors
html:
Charting tools analyze; broker platforms execute. This lesson tours the platforms that actually hold your money and route your orders, with a deep look at thinkorswim — the most capable free retail platform — and a survey of the other major brokers so you can choose deliberately.

Why brokers differ from charting tools

A broker is regulated, holds your cash and positions, and has a direct pipe to the exchanges. That changes its priorities: it cares about order types, fills, margin, account data, and reliability far more than about pretty drawings. Many traders analyze in TradingView and execute in a broker platform, accepting two windows in exchange for the best of each.

thinkorswim: the power user's free platform

thinkorswim (now under Charles Schwab) is a downloadable desktop application aimed at active and options traders. Its standout pieces:

  • The Scan tab is a full market scanner. You stack filters (price, volume, technical studies, even options-specific criteria) and save scans, much like the TradingView Screener but wired directly to your tradeable account.
  • The Analyze tab models options positions: risk graphs, probabilities, and how a spread behaves as price and time and volatility change. Nothing in TradingView matches it for options.
  • OnDemand replays historical market data so you can practice trading any past day as if it were live.
  • thinkScript, its own scripting language for custom studies and scan filters — the analog of Pine Script.
Tip.
thinkScript and Pine Script solve the same problem in different dialects. Concepts transfer: both have a bar-by-bar series model, both have technical-analysis functions, both let you build alerts and scans. Learn one deeply and the other is mostly syntax translation.

A taste of thinkScript

For contrast with Pine, here is the same idea — flag when a fast average crosses above a slow one — written as a thinkScript study:

MaCross.ts (thinkScript)
# thinkScript custom study: MA crossover signal input fastLength = 20; input slowLength = 50; def fast = Average(close, fastLength); def slow = Average(close, slowLength); plot FastLine = fast; plot SlowLine = slow; # crosses() is thinkScript's crossover helper plot CrossUp = crosses(fast, slow, CrossingDirection.ABOVE); CrossUp.SetPaintingStrategy(PaintingStrategy.BOOLEAN_ARROW_UP);

Notice the shared shape: inputs, definitions with a moving average, plots, and a crossover function. If lesson 16.5 made sense, this reads almost like a translation. You can drop this into the Scan tab's filter so the platform returns every symbol crossing up right now.

The major brokers compared

BrokerStrengthBest for
thinkorswim (Schwab)Free, deep options tools, thinkScriptActive & options traders
Interactive BrokersLowest costs, global markets, robust APIPros, algos, international
TastytradeOptions-first design, low feesPremium-selling options traders
Fidelity / Schwab (web)Reliability, research, custodyInvestors & long-term holders
Webull / RobinhoodSimple mobile-first UXBeginners, casual trading

Choosing without regret

Match the broker to what you actually trade. If you sell options spreads, thinkorswim or Tastytrade. If you want to run algorithms or trade global futures, Interactive Brokers' API is the standard. If you mostly hold ETFs, a mainstream custodian like Fidelity is plenty. Do not chase a flashy interface; chase fills, regulation, the products you need, and a paper account you can practice in first.

Watch out.
If you ever automate a broker via its API, your API keys can move real money. Never paste keys into a chat, a public repo, or a community script. Store them in environment variables or a secrets manager, scope keys to the minimum permissions, and start every automation on a paper account. A leaked live key is a leaked bank login.
Principle.
Your broker is a regulated financial institution holding your money; your charting tool is not. Verify regulatory registration, insurance (in the US, SIPC), and a real paper-trading mode before you fund any account.
▸Try It

Goal: get hands-on with a real broker platform's analysis tools — risk-free.

  1. Open a paper-trading account in thinkorswim (or your broker's simulator).
  2. In the Scan tab, build a scan for stocks above their 50-day average with volume over one million.
  3. Paste the MaCross.ts study into a custom study and apply it to a chart.
  4. Use OnDemand (or your broker's replay) to step through a past trading day and practice placing simulated orders.

title: Assembling Your Workflow: Watchlist → Alert → Journal
html:
You now know the tools. The final skill is wiring them into a single repeatable workflow so that, day after day, ideas flow from the whole market down to a logged, reviewed trade. A great workflow is what separates a disciplined trader from someone who reacts to whatever is flashing. This lesson assembles the pipeline and adds the two pieces beginners always skip: paper trading and a journal.

The end-to-end pipeline

Everything from the previous lessons snaps into one chain. Each stage narrows the funnel and hands off to the next:

daily_workflow.txt
# The repeatable loop, run every session 1. SCREEN whole market --> ~20 candidates (Screener / Scan) 2. WATCHLIST 20 candidates --> ~5 focus names (rank by quality) 3. ANALYZE 5 names --> define the setup (chart + levels) 4. ALERT arm price/indicator alerts at trigger levels 5. WAIT go live your life; let the alert find you 6. EXECUTE on ping: re-check, size by risk, place order in BROKER 7. MANAGE stop + target set the moment you enter 8. JOURNAL log entry, reason, screenshot, emotion, outcome 9. REVIEW weekly: read the journal, find your patterns |____________________________________________| feeds back into step 1

The loop closes: your weekly review of the journal tells you which screens and setups actually work, which sharpens next week's screening. Without the journal, step 9 is impossible and you never improve.

Paper trading first — always

Before a single dollar is at risk, run the entire pipeline in a simulator. TradingView has built-in Paper Trading (select "Paper Trading" as the broker in the trading panel), and every serious broker offers a paper account. Paper trading exists to test two things at once: whether your setup has an edge, and whether you can execute it without fumbling the platform.

Principle.
Backtesting validates the idea on the past; paper trading validates it going forward, in real time, with your own hands on the controls. Do both, in that order, before risking real capital. Skipping the paper-trading stage is the most expensive shortcut in trading.
Tip.
Paper trading has one honest limitation: it cannot simulate the emotion of real money or true fill slippage on illiquid names. Treat consistent paper success as permission to start small with real money, not as proof you have arrived. Size up only as live results confirm the paper results.

The journal: the highest-ROI habit

A trading journal is a structured record of every trade and the thinking behind it. It converts random experience into searchable data about your own behavior. The minimum useful columns:

FieldWhy it matters
Date / symbol / directionThe basic identity of the trade
Setup nameLets you group and rank strategies
Entry, stop, target, sizeProves you planned the risk first
Reason for entryCatches "I was bored" trades
ScreenshotThe chart at the moment of decision
Emotion / discipline noteSurfaces psychological leaks
Outcome & R-multipleResult measured in risk units

Logging in R-multiples — profit or loss measured as a multiple of the amount you risked — is the trick that makes trades comparable. A +2R win and a -1R loss tell you more than dollar amounts, because they normalize for position size. Over fifty trades, your average R per setup reveals which strategies actually pay.

Hands-on.
Connect the chain with TradingView's webhook alerts. A webhook fires an HTTP POST to a URL you control when the alert triggers, so an alert can automatically append a row to a journaling spreadsheet or notify a Discord channel. This is the bridge from manual to automated workflow — and the same mechanism that, pointed at a broker's API, can place real orders. Start it pointed only at a logging endpoint.

Putting it together

Your finished system is small and boring, which is the point. One saved screen, one focus watchlist, a handful of armed alerts, a paper account to rehearse in, a broker to execute in, and a journal to learn from. The tools we toured across this part are not the edge — the disciplined loop you run them in is.

Watch out.
The temptation is to add more tools, more indicators, more screens. Resist it. A workflow you actually follow every day beats a sophisticated one you abandon by Friday. Complexity is where discipline goes to die.
Tip.
None of this is financial advice. It is a process for learning and managing risk. Paper-trade first, protect your API keys, size positions so a string of losses cannot hurt you, and treat every alert as a prompt to think — never as an instruction to trade.
▸Try It

Goal: run one full loop of your own workflow end to end, on paper.

  1. Run your saved morning screen and pick five focus names into a watchlist.
  2. For one name, define entry, stop, and target on the chart, then arm an alert at the entry level.
  3. When (or if) it triggers, execute the trade in a paper account and immediately set the stop and target.
  4. Journal the trade with a screenshot, your reason, and the planned R-multiple — then schedule a weekly review of the journal.
